questions.q: Which god gave the Akshaya Patra to Yudhishthira at the start of the forest exile?
questions.explanation: Surya, the sun god, appeared before Yudhishthira and granted him the inexhaustible vessel.

questions.q: What did the Akshaya Patra provide for the exiled Pandavas?
questions.explanation: The vessel yielded food without limit every day, allowing the exiles to feed all who came to them.

questions.q: The vessel ceased yielding food for the day once which person had taken her meal?
questions.explanation: Its bounty lasted each day only until Draupadi herself had eaten, after which it gave nothing more until the next day.

questions.q: During which period of their lives did the Pandavas depend on the Akshaya Patra?
options: Their twelve years of forest exile
questions.explanation: The vessel sustained the household through the long forest years that followed the dice game.

questions.q: What does the word akshaya mean?
questions.explanation: Akshaya means undecaying or inexhaustible, naming the vessel for its endless bounty.

questions.q: Of what material is the Akshaya Patra usually said to have been made?
questions.explanation: The sun god's gift is traditionally described as a copper vessel.

questions.q: Who saved the Pandavas when Durvasa arrived for a meal after Draupadi had already eaten?
questions.explanation: Krishna appeared at Draupadi's desperate prayer and turned the impossible demand into a miracle.

questions.q: What single morsel did Krishna find clinging to the vessel?
questions.explanation: One leftover grain of rice remained in the vessel, and Krishna accepted and ate it.

questions.q: Who arranged for Durvasa to visit the Pandavas at the worst possible hour?
questions.explanation: Duryodhana asked the sage to call on the exiles after Draupadi had eaten, when the vessel would be empty.

questions.q: Why did Yudhishthira need a source of endless food in the forest?
options: Brahmins and followers had come into exile with him
Forest demons demanded a daily tribute of food
Bhima's hunger had doubled under a curse
He had vowed to feed every passing traveller
questions.explanation: Crowds of brahmins followed the Pandavas into exile, and Yudhishthira grieved that he had no means to feed them.

questions.q: On whose advice did Yudhishthira worship the sun god to obtain the means of feeding his people?
questions.explanation: Dhaumya counselled him that Surya is the source of all food and taught him how to praise the god.

questions.q: What happened the moment Krishna ate the remnant from the vessel?
options: Every hungry being in creation felt fed
The vessel overflowed with fresh food
The grain multiplied into a mountain of rice
questions.explanation: When Krishna, the soul of the universe, was satisfied, every stomach in creation, including those of Durvasa's party, felt full.

questions.q: Where were Durvasa and his disciples when their hunger suddenly left them?
questions.explanation: The sage and his disciples had gone to the river to bathe before the meal, and there their appetites vanished.

questions.q: Why did Durvasa's party slip away without returning for their meal?
options: They were full and feared insulting their hosts by refusing food
Bhima confronted them on the riverbank
A sudden storm scattered them downstream
Durvasa was summoned away by Indra
questions.explanation: Unable to eat another morsel, they fled rather than scorn a meal prepared for them and provoke the Pandavas' displeasure.

questions.q: How many disciples are commonly said to have accompanied Durvasa to the Pandavas' hermitage?
questions.explanation: Durvasa is traditionally said to have arrived with ten thousand disciples, all expecting to be fed.

questions.q: What did Duryodhana hope would happen when Durvasa reached the Pandavas after Draupadi's meal?
options: The sage would expose the Pandavas' hidden allies
The unfed sage's wrath would blast the Pandavas
The sage would demand the Akshaya Patra as a gift
The sage would end the exile with a blessing
questions.explanation: Duryodhana counted on the irascible sage cursing the brothers when they failed to feed his enormous following.

questions.q: How had Duryodhana earned the right to ask a favour of Durvasa?
options: By serving the sage and his disciples with flawless hospitality
By defeating the sage's rivals in debate
By gifting him a thousand cows and gold
By building him a hermitage on the Ganga
questions.explanation: Durvasa, pleased with Duryodhana's patient hospitality in Hastinapura, offered him a boon, which he turned into a trap.

questions.q: To whom did Draupadi cry out when Durvasa's arrival threatened the family with a curse?
questions.explanation: Draupadi prayed to Krishna in her helplessness, and he appeared at once, asking to be fed first.

questions.q: In which book of the Mahabharata does the story of the Akshaya Patra unfold?
questions.explanation: The vessel and the Durvasa episode belong to the Vana Parva, the book of the forest exile.

questions.q: How did Yudhishthira praise Surya to win the vessel?
options: With a year-long fast facing the sun
With a sacrifice of a hundred jars of ghee
With a hymn of the sun's one hundred and eight names
questions.explanation: Yudhishthira recited the one hundred and eight names of the sun, a hymn taught to him by Dhaumya.

questions.q: Why was Draupadi always the last to eat from the Akshaya Patra?
options: A vow bound her to eat only after sunset
She served her husbands and every guest before herself
Surya had commanded that the giver eat last
She fasted by day for her husbands' safety
questions.explanation: As mistress of the household Draupadi fed her husbands, the brahmins, and all guests before taking her own meal.

questions.q: What did Krishna do first when he appeared while Durvasa's disciples were at the river?
options: He hid the empty vessel from sight
He asked Draupadi to bring him food, saying he was hungry
He sent Bhima to delay the sage
He refilled the vessel with a touch
questions.explanation: To Draupadi's dismay Krishna declared himself hungry and insisted she bring him the vessel at once.

questions.q: For how long did Surya promise that the vessel would supply the Pandavas?
questions.explanation: Surya said the vessel would feed them through their twelve years in the forest, the span of the open exile.

questions.q: Durvasa is traditionally regarded as born from the essence of which deity?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds Durvasa to be born of Shiva's wrathful essence, which explains his fearsome temper.

questions.q: What made an unsatisfied Durvasa so dangerous a guest?
options: His curses, which had broken kings and gods alike
His authority to extend the exile
His kinship with the Kaurava court
questions.explanation: Durvasa's curses were legendary, having struck down figures as great as Shakuntala and Indra himself.

questions.q: Along with the grain of rice, what is commonly said to have clung to the vessel's rim?
questions.explanation: In the common retelling Krishna found a grain of rice with a scrap of vegetable, and that morsel sufficed.

questions.q: What did Krishna declare after swallowing the remnant from the vessel?
options: Let no guest leave this forest hungry
May this hermitage never know famine
May the soul of the universe be satisfied
questions.explanation: Krishna prayed that the soul of the universe be satisfied by the morsel, and the satisfaction spread to all beings.

questions.q: In which forest were the Pandavas living when Durvasa came at Duryodhana's bidding?
questions.explanation: The episode is set in the Kamyaka forest, where the exiles kept their hermitage at the time.

questions.q: Besides food, what did Surya promise Yudhishthira when granting the vessel?
options: That Karna would one day spare him
That Draupadi's honour would be avenged
That no guest would ever curse his table
That he would regain his kingdom in the fourteenth year
questions.explanation: Surya foretold that after the years of exile Yudhishthira would recover his kingdom in the fourteenth year.

questions.q: What kinds of food did the Akshaya Patra yield each day?
questions.explanation: The epic describes the vessel yielding the four kinds of food, fruits, roots, meat, and vegetables, in endless measure.
